 title: Retire callTool/DirectToolCall escape hatch — native tool-calling subsumes its purpose
 ---
 ## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`. Deleting `DirectToolCall.swift` in this task WILL break `MultiToolAgent.swift`'s compile unless you also update it here: remove `dispatchCallTool`/the `callTool` turn-handling branch (and its `directTools` plumbing/constructor parameter) from `MultiToolAgent` now, leaving it building and passing its existing tests with `callTool` support gone — `MultiToolAgent.swift` itself is fully deleted later in `7840f24`, but it must compile and its remaining tests must pass at the end of *this* task.

If a caller wants a tool NOT meant for JS-snippet composition (rare — most wrapped tools are meant to be called from `runCode`), the answer going forward is: don't route it through `MultiTool`'s registry at all — register it as its own separate `Tool` directly with `LanguageModelSession(tools:)`, alongside `multiTool` and `findAPIsTool`. No dedicated Multitool-side mechanism is needed for this.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `DirectToolCall.swift` and its dedicated test file are deleted.
- [ ] `MultiToolAgent.swift` is updated (not just left broken) to compile and pass its existing tests with the `callTool` turn-handling path, `directTools` plumbing, and `ToolDescriptions.callTool` removed.
- [ ] No remaining references to `callTool`/`DirectToolCall`/`directTools` exist outside historical doc-comment mentions (e.g. "formerly supported callTool") — grep confirms no live code path.
- [ ] `MultiTool.Registry`'s public surface (`affordances`, `isDirectMode`, `supportsFindAPIs`) is reviewed: if any of it existed only to support `callTool`, remove it; if it's purely about `findAPIs` vs. direct/no-discovery mode, keep it (that distinction is independent of this decision).
- [ ] `swift build` and full `swift test` remain green.

## Tests
- [ ] Confirm no test file references `DirectToolCall`/`callTool` after deletion (`grep -rn "DirectToolCall\|callTool" Sources/ Tests/` returns only historical prose, if anything).
- [ ] `MultiToolAgentTests.swift`'s existing coverage (minus whatever tests were specifically about `callTool`, which should be removed) still passes.
- [ ] Full `swift test` passes with no regressions.

## Workflow
- Use `/tdd` where applicable — this is primarily a deletion task; verify the full suite stays green after removal, and add/adjust any test asserting `MultiTool.Registry`'s surviving affordance metadata (e.g. `affordances == ["runCode", "findAPIs"]` without a `callTool` entry) if such a test doesn't already exist independent of `callTool`.

## Review Findings (2026-07-06 10:23)

- [x] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TranscriptAnalyzer.swift:250` — The `isRunCode` and `isFindAPIs` computed properties on the `AgentStep` extension are verbatim near-duplicates. Both use identical code structure differing only by the enum case name being checked.  title: Retire callTool/DirectToolCall escape hatch — native tool-calling subsumes its purpose
 ---
 ## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`. Deleting `DirectToolCall.swift` in this task WILL break `MultiToolAgent.swift`'s compile unless you also update it here: remove `dispatchCallTool`/the `callTool` turn-handling branch (and its `directTools` plumbing/constructor parameter) from `MultiToolAgent` now, leaving it building and passing its existing tests with `callTool` support gone — `MultiToolAgent.swift` itself is fully deleted later in `7840f24`, but it must compile and its remaining tests must pass at the end of *this* task.

If a caller wants a tool NOT meant for JS-snippet composition (rare — most wrapped tools are meant to be called from `runCode`), the answer going forward is: don't route it through `MultiTool`'s registry at all — register it as its own separate `Tool` directly with `LanguageModelSession(tools:)`, alongside `multiTool` and `findAPIsTool`. No dedicated Multitool-side mechanism is needed for this.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `DirectToolCall.swift` and its dedicated test file are deleted.
- [ ] `MultiToolAgent.swift` is updated (not just left broken) to compile and pass its existing tests with the `callTool` turn-handling path, `directTools` plumbing, and `ToolDescriptions.callTool` removed.
- [ ] No remaining references to `callTool`/`DirectToolCall`/`directTools` exist outside historical doc-comment mentions (e.g. "formerly supported callTool") — grep confirms no live code path.
- [ ] `MultiTool.Registry`'s public surface (`affordances`, `isDirectMode`, `supportsFindAPIs`) is reviewed: if any of it existed only to support `callTool`, remove it; if it's purely about `findAPIs` vs. direct/no-discovery mode, keep it (that distinction is independent of this decision).
- [ ] `swift build` and full `swift test` remain green.

## Tests
- [ ] Confirm no test file references `DirectToolCall`/`callTool` after deletion (`grep -rn "DirectToolCall\|callTool" Sources/ Tests/` returns only historical prose, if anything).
- [ ] `MultiToolAgentTests.swift`'s existing coverage (minus whatever tests were specifically about `callTool`, which should be removed) still passes.
- [ ] Full `swift test` passes with no regressions.

## Workflow
- Use `/tdd` where applicable — this is primarily a deletion task; verify the full suite stays green after removal, and add/adjust any test asserting `MultiTool.Registry`'s surviving affordance metadata (e.g. `affordances == ["runCode", "findAPIs"]` without a `callTool` entry) if such a test doesn't already exist independent of `callTool`.

 position_ordinal: '80'
 title: Retire callTool/DirectToolCall escape hatch — native tool-calling subsumes its purpose
 ---
-## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`. Deleting `DirectToolCall.swift` in this task WILL break `MultiToolAgent.swift`'s compile unless you also update it here: remove `dispatchCallTool`/the `callTool` turn-handling branch (and its `directTools` plumbing/constructor parameter) from `MultiToolAgent` now, leaving it building and passing its existing tests with `callTool` support gone — `MultiToolAgent.swift` itself is fully deleted later in `7840f24`, but it must compile and its remaining tests must pass at the end of *this* task.

If a caller wants a tool NOT meant for JS-snippet composition (rare — most wrapped tools are meant to be called from `runCode`), the answer going forward is: don't route it through `MultiTool`'s registry at all — register it as its own separate `Tool` directly with `LanguageModelSession(tools:)`, alongside `multiTool` and `findAPIsTool`. No dedicated Multitool-side mechanism is needed for this.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `DirectToolCall.swift` and its dedicated test file are deleted.
- [ ] `MultiToolAgent.swift` is updated (not just left broken) to compile and pass its existing tests with the `callTool` turn-handling path, `directTools` plumbing, and `ToolDescriptions.callTool` removed.
- [ ] No remaining references to `callTool`/`DirectToolCall`/`directTools` exist outside historical doc-comment mentions (e.g. "formerly supported callTool") — grep confirms no live code path.
- [ ] `MultiTool.Registry`'s public surface (`affordances`, `isDirectMode`, `supportsFindAPIs`) is reviewed: if any of it existed only to support `callTool`, remove it; if it's purely about `findAPIs` vs. direct/no-discovery mode, keep it (that distinction is independent of this decision).
- [ ] `swift build` and full `swift test` remain green.

## Tests
- [ ] Confirm no test file references `DirectToolCall`/`callTool` after deletion (`grep -rn "DirectToolCall\|callTool" Sources/ Tests/` returns only historical prose, if anything).
- [ ] `MultiToolAgentTests.swift`'s existing coverage (minus whatever tests were specifically about `callTool`, which should be removed) still passes.
- [ ] Full `swift test` passes with no regressions.

## Workflow
- Use `/tdd` where applicable — this is primarily a deletion task; verify the full suite stays green after removal, and add/adjust any test asserting `MultiTool.Registry`'s surviving affordance metadata (e.g. `affordances == ["runCode", "findAPIs"]` without a `callTool` entry) if such a test doesn't already exist independent of `callTool`.

## Review Findings (2026-07-06 10:23)

- [x] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TranscriptAnalyzer.swift:250` — The `isRunCode` and `isFindAPIs` computed properties on the `AgentStep` extension are verbatim near-duplicates. Both use identical code structure differing only by the enum case name being checked. While Swift idiomatically repeats this pattern per case, the duplication could be reduced by extracting a generic helper: `fileprivate func `is`(_ casePredicate: (Self) -> Bool) -> Bool { casePredicate(self) }`, then `var isRunCode: Bool { `is`(/AgentStep.runCode != nil) }` — though this trades clarity for DRY. Accept as a language-forced idiom if clarity is prioritized.
- [x] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TurnFormat.swift:107` — ActionVerb and ActionName are near-duplicate enum definitions with identical structure but different literal values. Both enumerate the same three cases (findAPIs, runCode, final) — one lowercased, one proper-cased — creating parallel definitions that must be kept in sync. Consolidate into a single enum with computed properties: `private enum Action: String { case findAPIs; case runCode; case final }` with `var lowercased: String { rawValue.lowercased() }`, then use `Action.findAPIs.rawValue` where ActionName is used and `Action.findAPIs.lowercased` where ActionVerb is used.
- [x] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:365` — The `describe(_:)` function is a needless helper with a single call site. It wraps only a switch statement that could be inlined into its sole caller without loss of clarity. Inline the switch statement directly into the `compactMap` closure in `traceLines`, eliminating the intermediate function call.

Define flags as structured data and reference from all locations: switch dispatch, usage text, and error messages should all interpret the same flag definitions.

--- original
+++ modified
@@ -149,4 +149,4 @@
 position_ordinal: '80'
 title: Retire callTool/DirectToolCall escape hatch — native tool-calling subsumes its purpose
 ---
-## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`. Deleting `DirectToolCall.swift` in this task WILL break `MultiToolAgent.swift`'s compile unless you also update it here: remove `dispatchCallTool`/the `callTool` turn-handling branch (and its `directTools` plumbing/constructor parameter) from `MultiToolAgent` now, leaving it building and passing its existing tests with `callTool` support gone — `MultiToolAgent.swift` itself is fully deleted later in `7840f24`, but it must compile and its remaining tests must pass at the end of *this* task.

If a caller wants a tool NOT meant for JS-snippet composition (rare — most wrapped tools are meant to be called from `runCode`), the answer going forward is: don't route it through `MultiTool`'s registry at all — register it as its own separate `Tool` directly with `LanguageModelSession(tools:)`, alongside `multiTool` and `findAPIsTool`. No dedicated Multitool-side mechanism is needed for this.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `DirectToolCall.swift` and its dedicated test file are deleted.
- [ ] `MultiToolAgent.swift` is updated (not just left broken) to compile and pass its existing tests with the `callTool` turn-handling path, `directTools` plumbing, and `ToolDescriptions.callTool` removed.
- [ ] No remaining references to `callTool`/`DirectToolCall`/`directTools` exist outside historical doc-comment mentions (e.g. "formerly supported callTool") — grep confirms no live code path.
- [ ] `MultiTool.Registry`'s public surface (`affordances`, `isDirectMode`, `supportsFindAPIs`) is reviewed: if any of it existed only to support `callTool`, remove it; if it's purely about `findAPIs` vs. direct/no-discovery mode, keep it (that distinction is independent of this decision).
- [ ] `swift build` and full `swift test` remain green.

## Tests
- [ ] Confirm no test file references `DirectToolCall`/`callTool` after deletion (`grep -rn "DirectToolCall\|callTool" Sources/ Tests/` returns only historical prose, if anything).
- [ ] `MultiToolAgentTests.swift`'s existing coverage (minus whatever tests were specifically about `callTool`, which should be removed) still passes.
- [ ] Full `swift test` passes with no regressions.

## Workflow
- Use `/tdd` where applicable — this is primarily a deletion task; verify the full suite stays green after removal, and add/adjust any test asserting `MultiTool.Registry`'s surviving affordance metadata (e.g. `affordances == ["runCode", "findAPIs"]` without a `callTool` entry) if such a test doesn't already exist independent of `callTool`.

Both use identical code structure differing only by the enum case name being checked. While Swift idiomatically repeats this pattern per case, the duplication could be reduced by extracting a generic helper: `fileprivate func `is`(_ casePredicate: (Self) -> Bool) -> Bool { casePredicate(self) }`, then `var isRunCode: Bool { `is`(/AgentStep.runCode != nil) }` — though this trades clarity for DRY. Accept as a language-forced idiom if clarity is prioritized.

Both enumerate the same three cases (findAPIs, runCode, final) — one lowercased, one proper-cased — creating parallel definitions that must be kept in sync. Consolidate into a single enum with computed properties: `private enum Action: String { case findAPIs; case runCode; case final }` with `var lowercased: String { rawValue.lowercased() }`, then use `Action.findAPIs.rawValue` where ActionName is used and `Action.findAPIs.lowercased` where ActionVerb is used.

## Review Findings (2026-07-06 11:46)

- [ ] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TranscriptAnalyzer.swift:154` — Property name `isFindAPIs` uses inconsistent acronym capitalization. Modern Swift style requires camelCase even for acronyms, so 'APIs' should be 'Apis'. Rename to `isFindApis` to match Swift camelCase conventions and align with the pattern used by `isRunCode`.
- [ ] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TranscriptAnalyzer.swift:163` — The `else` branch of the `guard let` is unreachable because the regex pattern is a hardcoded, validated literal. The `preconditionFailure` on line 164 will never execute, even though it is documented as an intentional invariant check. Replace the `guard let ... else` with `let regex = try! NSRegularExpression(pattern: pattern)` to make the invariant explicit. Add a comment explaining that the pattern is valid by construction and any error indicates a broken invariant.
- [ ] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:106` — The flag name `"--help"` is hardcoded in the error message string, duplicating the flag definition in the switch statement and usage text. This literal should be derived from a single source of truth. Reference the flag from a shared data structure, or use a named constant `private let helpFlagName = "--help"` at minimum.
- [ ] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:178` — The flag literal `"--direct"` is hardcoded in the switch case, but also appears in the usage text (lines 113–114), requiring maintenance across multiple locations when the flag name changes. CLI flags should be defined as data once. Define CLI flags as a data structure: `struct Flag { let names: [String]; let description: String; let setter: (inout CLIArguments) -> Void }` and generate both the switch statement and the usage text from this array.
- [ ] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:180` — The flag literals `"--help"` and `"-h"` are hardcoded in the switch case, but repeat in the usage text (lines 113, 118) and error message (line 106), requiring synchronization across four locations. This violates the data-driven principle. Define flags as structured data and reference from all locations: switch dispatch, usage text, and error messages should all interpret the same flag definitions.

\ No newline at end of file
+## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`. Deleting `DirectToolCall.swift` in this task WILL break `MultiToolAgent.swift`'s compile unless you also update it here: remove `dispatchCallTool`/the `callTool` turn-handling branch (and its `directTools` plumbing/constructor parameter) from `MultiToolAgent` now, leaving it building and passing its existing tests with `callTool` support gone — `MultiToolAgent.swift` itself is fully deleted later in `7840f24`, but it must compile and its remaining tests must pass at the end of *this* task.

If a caller wants a tool NOT meant for JS-snippet composition (rare — most wrapped tools are meant to be called from `runCode`), the answer going forward is: don't route it through `MultiTool`'s registry at all — register it as its own separate `Tool` directly with `LanguageModelSession(tools:)`, alongside `multiTool` and `findAPIsTool`. No dedicated Multitool-side mechanism is needed for this.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `DirectToolCall.swift` and its dedicated test file are deleted.
- [ ] `MultiToolAgent.swift` is updated (not just left broken) to compile and pass its existing tests with the `callTool` turn-handling path, `directTools` plumbing, and `ToolDescriptions.callTool` removed.
- [ ] No remaining references to `callTool`/`DirectToolCall`/`directTools` exist outside historical doc-comment mentions (e.g. "formerly supported callTool") — grep confirms no live code path.
- [ ] `MultiTool.Registry`'s public surface (`affordances`, `isDirectMode`, `supportsFindAPIs`) is reviewed: if any of it existed only to support `callTool`, remove it; if it's purely about `findAPIs` vs. direct/no-discovery mode, keep it (that distinction is independent of this decision).
- [ ] `swift build` and full `swift test` remain green.

## Tests
- [ ] Confirm no test file references `DirectToolCall`/`callTool` after deletion (`grep -rn "DirectToolCall\|callTool" Sources/ Tests/` returns only historical prose, if anything).
- [ ] `MultiToolAgentTests.swift`'s existing coverage (minus whatever tests were specifically about `callTool`, which should be removed) still passes.
- [ ] Full `swift test` passes with no regressions.

## Workflow
- Use `/tdd` where applicable — this is primarily a deletion task; verify the full suite stays green after removal, and add/adjust any test asserting `MultiTool.Registry`'s surviving affordance metadata (e.g. `affordances == ["runCode", "findAPIs"]` without a `callTool` entry) if such a test doesn't already exist independent of `callTool`.

 title: Retire callTool/DirectToolCall escape hatch — native tool-calling subsumes its purpose
 ---
 ## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`. Deleting `DirectToolCall.swift` in this task WILL break `MultiToolAgent.swift`'s compile unless you also update it here: remove `dispatchCallTool`/the `callTool` turn-handling branch (and its `directTools` plumbing/constructor parameter) from `MultiToolAgent` now, leaving it building and passing its existing tests with `callTool` support gone — `MultiToolAgent.swift` itself is fully deleted later in `7840f24`, but it must compile and its remaining tests must pass at the end of *this* task.

If a caller wants a tool NOT meant for JS-snippet composition (rare — most wrapped tools are meant to be called from `runCode`), the answer going forward is: don't route it through `MultiTool`'s registry at all — register it as its own separate `Tool` directly with `LanguageModelSession(tools:)`, alongside `multiTool` and `findAPIsTool`. No dedicated Multitool-side mechanism is needed for this.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `DirectToolCall.swift` and its dedicated test file are deleted.
- [ ] `MultiToolAgent.swift` is updated (not just left broken) to compile and pass its existing tests with the `callTool` turn-handling path, `directTools` plumbing, and `ToolDescriptions.callTool` removed.
- [ ] No remaining references to `callTool`/`DirectToolCall`/`directTools` exist outside historical doc-comment mentions (e.g. "formerly supported callTool") — grep confirms no live code path.
- [ ] `MultiTool.Registry`'s public surface (`affordances`, `isDirectMode`, `supportsFindAPIs`) is reviewed: if any of it existed only to support `callTool`, remove it; if it's purely about `findAPIs` vs. direct/no-discovery mode, keep it (that distinction is independent of this decision).
- [ ] `swift build` and full `swift test` remain green.

## Tests
- [ ] Confirm no test file references `DirectToolCall`/`callTool` after deletion (`grep -rn "DirectToolCall\|callTool" Sources/ Tests/` returns only historical prose, if anything).
- [ ] `MultiToolAgentTests.swift`'s existing coverage (minus whatever tests were specifically about `callTool`, which should be removed) still passes.
- [ ] Full `swift test` passes with no regressions.

## Workflow
- Use `/tdd` where applicable — this is primarily a deletion task; verify the full suite stays green after removal, and add/adjust any test asserting `MultiTool.Registry`'s surviving affordance metadata (e.g. `affordances == ["runCode", "findAPIs"]` without a `callTool` entry) if such a test doesn't already exist independent of `callTool`.

## Review Findings (2026-07-06 10:23)

- [x] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TranscriptAnalyzer.swift:250` — The `isRunCode` and `isFindAPIs` computed properties on the `AgentStep` extension are verbatim near-duplicates. Both use identical code structure differing only by the enum case name being checked. While Swift idiomatically repeats this pattern per case, the duplication could be reduced by extracting a generic helper: `fileprivate func `is`(_ casePredicate: (Self) -> Bool) -> Bool { casePredicate(self) }`, then `var isRunCode: Bool { `is`(/AgentStep.runCode != nil) }` — though this trades clarity for DRY. Accept as a language-forced idiom if clarity is prioritized.
- [x] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TurnFormat.swift:107` — ActionVerb and ActionName are near-duplicate enum definitions with identical structure but different literal values. Both enumerate the same three cases (findAPIs, runCode, final) — one lowercased, one proper-cased — creating parallel definitions that must be kept in sync. Consolidate into a single enum with computed properties: `private enum Action: String { case findAPIs; case runCode; case final }` with `var lowercased: String { rawValue.lowercased() }`, then use `Action.findAPIs.rawValue` where ActionName is used and `Action.findAPIs.lowercased` where ActionVerb is used.
- [x] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:365` — The `describe(_:)` function is a needless helper with a single call site. It wraps only a switch statement that could be inlined into its sole caller without loss of clarity. Inline the switch statement directly into the `compactMap` closure in `traceLines`, eliminating the intermediate function call.

- [x] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TurnFormat.swift:325` — `extractRest`/`extractCode` line-concatenation duplication. Fixed: extracted a shared private `joinFieldValue(_:withLinesFrom:startIndex:trimmed:)` helper parameterized by the field value, the starting line index, and whether to trim the joined result; both `extractCode`'s no-fence fallback and `extractRest` now delegate to it instead of hand-rolling the same array-build/join logic. Verified behavior-preserving (adversarial double-check confirmed the guard conditions and trim behavior are unchanged for both callers).
- [x] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:38` — First line of doc comment is incomplete. Fixed: `CLIArgumentError.description`'s doc first line is now 'A human-readable description of the error.', with the `CustomStringConvertible` note moved to elaboration.

During the fix, a full top-to-bottom sweep (not just the cited lines, per the task's explicit instruction) turned up several additional doc comments in `TurnFormat.swift` with the same incomplete-first-line pattern that no review round had ever cited: the `AgentStep` enum's own type doc, `TurnParseError.message`'s doc, the `TurnFormat` protocol's `maxRepairTurns` and `formatInstructions` doc comments, and the `TolerantParseTurnFormat` struct's own type doc. All were rewritten to the same complete-sentence-first-line-then-blank-then-elaboration shape. `swift build` and `swift test` (237 tests passed, gated suites skipped as before) confirmed green after all fixes, and an adversarial double-check agent independently verified every doc-comment first line across all three files, the `joinFieldValue` refactor's behavior-preservation, and re-ran build/test fresh — PASS, no blocking findings.

## Review Findings (2026-07-06 11:46)

- [ ] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TranscriptAnalyzer.swift:154` — Property name `isFindAPIs` uses inconsistent acronym capitalization. Modern Swift style requires camelCase even for acronyms, so 'APIs' should be 'Apis'. Rename to `isFindApis` to match Swift camelCase conventions and align with the pattern used by `isRunCode`.
- [ ] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TranscriptAnalyzer.swift:163` — The `else` branch of the `guard let` is unreachable because the regex pattern is a hardcoded, validated literal. The `preconditionFailure` on line 164 will never execute, even though it is documented as an intentional invariant check. Replace the `guard let ... else` with `let regex = try! NSRegularExpression(pattern: pattern)` to make the invariant explicit. Add a comment explaining that the pattern is valid by construction and any error indicates a broken invariant.
- [ ] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:106` — The flag name `"--help"` is hardcoded in the error message string, duplicating the flag definition in the switch statement and usage text. This literal should be derived from a single source of truth. Reference the flag from a shared data structure, or use a named constant `private let helpFlagName = "--help"` at minimum.
- [ ] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:178` — The flag literal `"--direct"` is hardcoded in the switch case, but also appears in the usage text (lines 113–114), requiring maintenance across multiple locations when the flag name changes. CLI flags should be defined as data once. Define CLI flags as a data structure: `struct Flag { let names: [String]; let description: String; let setter: (inout CLIArguments) -> Void }` and generate both the switch statement and the usage text from this array.
- [ ] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:180` — The flag literals `"--help"` and `"-h"` are hardcoded in the switch case, but repeat in the usage text (lines 113, 118) and error message (line 106), requiring synchronization across four locations. This violates the data-driven principle. Define flags as structured data and reference from all locations: switch dispatch, usage text, and error messages should all interpret the same flag definitions.

- [ ] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TranscriptAnalyzer.swift:163` — The `else` branch of the `guard let` is unreachable because the regex pattern is a hardcoded, validated literal. The `preconditionFailure` on line 164 will never execute, even though it is documented as an intentional invariant check. Replace the `guard let ... else` with `let regex = try! NSRegularExpression(pattern: pattern)` to make the invariant explicit. Add a comment explaining that the pattern is valid by construction and any error indicates a broken invariant.
- [ ] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:106` — The flag name `"--help"` is hardcoded in the error message string, duplicating the flag definition in the switch statement and usage text. This literal should be derived from a single source of truth. Reference the flag from a shared data structure, or use a named constant `private let helpFlagName = "--help"` at minimum.
- [ ] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:178` — The flag literal `"--direct"` is hardcoded in the switch case, but also appears in the usage text (lines 113–114), requiring maintenance across multiple locations when the flag name changes. CLI flags should be defined as data once. Define CLI flags as a data structure: `struct Flag { let names: [String]; let description: String; let setter: (inout CLIArguments) -> Void }` and generate both the switch statement and the usage text from this array.
- [ ] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:180` — The flag literals `"--help"` and `"-h"` are hardcoded in the switch case, but repeat in the usage text (lines 113, 118) and error message (line 106), requiring synchronization across four locations. This violates the data-driven principle. Define flags as structured data and reference from all locations: switch dispatch, usage text, and error messages should all interpret the same flag definitions.

forward_patch:
--- original
+++ modified
@@ -153,4 +153,4 @@
 position_ordinal: '8180'
 title: Retire callTool/DirectToolCall escape hatch — native tool-calling subsumes its purpose
 ---
-## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`. Deleting `DirectToolCall.swift` in this task WILL break `MultiToolAgent.swift`'s compile unless you also update it here: remove `dispatchCallTool`/the `callTool` turn-handling branch (and its `directTools` plumbing/constructor parameter) from `MultiToolAgent` now, leaving it building and passing its existing tests with `callTool` support gone — `MultiToolAgent.swift` itself is fully deleted later in `7840f24`, but it must compile and its remaining tests must pass at the end of *this* task.

If a caller wants a tool NOT meant for JS-snippet composition (rare — most wrapped tools are meant to be called from `runCode`), the answer going forward is: don't route it through `MultiTool`'s registry at all — register it as its own separate `Tool` directly with `LanguageModelSession(tools:)`, alongside `multiTool` and `findAPIsTool`. No dedicated Multitool-side mechanism is needed for this.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `DirectToolCall.swift` and its dedicated test file are deleted.
- [ ] `MultiToolAgent.swift` is updated (not just left broken) to compile and pass its existing tests with the `callTool` turn-handling path, `directTools` plumbing, and `ToolDescriptions.callTool` removed.
- [ ] No remaining references to `callTool`/`DirectToolCall`/`directTools` exist outside historical doc-comment mentions (e.g. "formerly supported callTool") — grep confirms no live code path.
- [ ] `MultiTool.Registry`'s public surface (`affordances`, `isDirectMode`, `supportsFindAPIs`) is reviewed: if any of it existed only to support `callTool`, remove it; if it's purely about `findAPIs` vs. direct/no-discovery mode, keep it (that distinction is independent of this decision).
- [ ] `swift build` and full `swift test` remain green.

## Tests
- [ ] Confirm no test file references `DirectToolCall`/`callTool` after deletion (`grep -rn "DirectToolCall\|callTool" Sources/ Tests/` returns only historical prose, if anything).
- [ ] `MultiToolAgentTests.swift`'s existing coverage (minus whatever tests were specifically about `callTool`, which should be removed) still passes.
- [ ] Full `swift test` passes with no regressions.

## Workflow
- Use `/tdd` where applicable — this is primarily a deletion task; verify the full suite stays green after removal, and add/adjust any test asserting `MultiTool.Registry`'s surviving affordance metadata (e.g. `affordances == ["runCode", "findAPIs"]` without a `callTool` entry) if such a test doesn't already exist independent of `callTool`.

## Review Findings (2026-07-06 10:23)

- [x] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TranscriptAnalyzer.swift:250` — The `isRunCode` and `isFindAPIs` computed properties on the `AgentStep` extension are verbatim near-duplicates. Both use identical code structure differing only by the enum case name being checked. While Swift idiomatically repeats this pattern per case, the duplication could be reduced by extracting a generic helper: `fileprivate func `is`(_ casePredicate: (Self) -> Bool) -> Bool { casePredicate(self) }`, then `var isRunCode: Bool { `is`(/AgentStep.runCode != nil) }` — though this trades clarity for DRY. Accept as a language-forced idiom if clarity is prioritized.
- [x] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TurnFormat.swift:107` — ActionVerb and ActionName are near-duplicate enum definitions with identical structure but different literal values. Both enumerate the same three cases (findAPIs, runCode, final) — one lowercased, one proper-cased — creating parallel definitions that must be kept in sync. Consolidate into a single enum with computed properties: `private enum Action: String { case findAPIs; case runCode; case final }` with `var lowercased: String { rawValue.lowercased() }`, then use `Action.findAPIs.rawValue` where ActionName is used and `Action.findAPIs.lowercased` where ActionVerb is used.
- [x] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:365` — The `describe(_:)` function is a needless helper with a single call site. It wraps only a switch statement that could be inlined into its sole caller without loss of clarity. Inline the switch statement directly into the `compactMap` closure in `traceLines`, eliminating the intermediate function call.

+## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`. Deleting `DirectToolCall.swift` in this task WILL break `MultiToolAgent.swift`'s compile unless you also update it here: remove `dispatchCallTool`/the `callTool` turn-handling branch (and its `directTools` plumbing/constructor parameter) from `MultiToolAgent` now, leaving it building and passing its existing tests with `callTool` support gone — `MultiToolAgent.swift` itself is fully deleted later in `7840f24`, but it must compile and its remaining tests must pass at the end of *this* task.

If a caller wants a tool NOT meant for JS-snippet composition (rare — most wrapped tools are meant to be called from `runCode`), the answer going forward is: don't route it through `MultiTool`'s registry at all — register it as its own separate `Tool` directly with `LanguageModelSession(tools:)`, alongside `multiTool` and `findAPIsTool`. No dedicated Multitool-side mechanism is needed for this.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `DirectToolCall.swift` and its dedicated test file are deleted.
- [ ] `MultiToolAgent.swift` is updated (not just left broken) to compile and pass its existing tests with the `callTool` turn-handling path, `directTools` plumbing, and `ToolDescriptions.callTool` removed.
- [ ] No remaining references to `callTool`/`DirectToolCall`/`directTools` exist outside historical doc-comment mentions (e.g. "formerly supported callTool") — grep confirms no live code path.
- [ ] `MultiTool.Registry`'s public surface (`affordances`, `isDirectMode`, `supportsFindAPIs`) is reviewed: if any of it existed only to support `callTool`, remove it; if it's purely about `findAPIs` vs. direct/no-discovery mode, keep it (that distinction is independent of this decision).
- [ ] `swift build` and full `swift test` remain green.

## Tests
- [ ] Confirm no test file references `DirectToolCall`/`callTool` after deletion (`grep -rn "DirectToolCall\|callTool" Sources/ Tests/` returns only historical prose, if anything).
- [ ] `MultiToolAgentTests.swift`'s existing coverage (minus whatever tests were specifically about `callTool`, which should be removed) still passes.
- [ ] Full `swift test` passes with no regressions.

## Workflow
- Use `/tdd` where applicable — this is primarily a deletion task; verify the full suite stays green after removal, and add/adjust any test asserting `MultiTool.Registry`'s surviving affordance metadata (e.g. `affordances == ["runCode", "findAPIs"]` without a `callTool` entry) if such a test doesn't already exist independent of `callTool`.

+## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`. Deleting `DirectToolCall.swift` in this task WILL break `MultiToolAgent.swift`'s compile unless you also update it here: remove `dispatchCallTool`/the `callTool` turn-handling branch (and its `directTools` plumbing/constructor parameter) from `MultiToolAgent` now, leaving it building and passing its existing tests with `callTool` support gone — `MultiToolAgent.swift` itself is fully deleted later in `7840f24`, but it must compile and its remaining tests must pass at the end of *this* task.

If a caller wants a tool NOT meant for JS-snippet composition (rare — most wrapped tools are meant to be called from `runCode`), the answer going forward is: don't route it through `MultiTool`'s registry at all — register it as its own separate `Tool` directly with `LanguageModelSession(tools:)`, alongside `multiTool` and `findAPIsTool`. No dedicated Multitool-side mechanism is needed for this.

- [ ] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TranscriptAnalyzer.swift:154` — Property name `isFindAPIs` uses inconsistent acronym capitalization. - [ ] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TranscriptAnalyzer.swift:163` — The `else` branch of the `guard let` is unreachable because the regex pattern is a hardcoded, validated literal. The `preconditionFailure` on line 164 will never execute, even though it is documented as an intentional invariant check. Replace the `guard let ... else` with `let regex = try! NSRegularExpression(pattern: pattern)` to make the invariant explicit. Add a comment explaining that the pattern is valid by construction and any error indicates a broken invariant.
- [ ] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:106` — The flag name `"--help"` is hardcoded in the error message string, duplicating the flag definition in the switch statement and usage text. This literal should be derived from a single source of truth. Reference the flag from a shared data structure, or use a named constant `private let helpFlagName = "--help"` at minimum.
- [ ] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:178` — The flag literal `"--direct"` is hardcoded in the switch case, but also appears in the usage text (lines 113–114), requiring maintenance across multiple locations when the flag name changes. CLI flags should be defined as data once. Define CLI flags as a data structure: `struct Flag { let names: [String]; let description: String; let setter: (inout CLIArguments) -> Void }` and generate both the switch statement and the usage text from this array.
- [ ] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:180` — The flag literals `"--help"` and `"-h"` are hardcoded in the switch case, but repeat in the usage text (lines 113, 118) and error message (line 106), requiring synchronization across four locations. This violates the data-driven principle. Define flags as structured data and reference from all locations: switch dispatch, usage text, and error messages should all interpret the same flag definitions.

 title: Retire callTool/DirectToolCall escape hatch — native tool-calling subsumes its purpose
 ---
 ## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`.  ## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

--- original
+++ modified
@@ -187,4 +187,4 @@
 position_ordinal: '80'
 title: Retire callTool/DirectToolCall escape hatch — native tool-calling subsumes its purpose
 ---
-## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`. Deleting `DirectToolCall.swift` in this task WILL break `MultiToolAgent.swift`'s compile unless you also update it here: remove `dispatchCallTool`/the `callTool` turn-handling branch (and its `directTools` plumbing/constructor parameter) from `MultiToolAgent` now, leaving it building and passing its existing tests with `callTool` support gone — `MultiToolAgent.swift` itself is fully deleted later in `7840f24`, but it must compile and its remaining tests must pass at the end of *this* task.

If a caller wants a tool NOT meant for JS-snippet composition (rare — most wrapped tools are meant to be called from `runCode`), the answer going forward is: don't route it through `MultiTool`'s registry at all — register it as its own separate `Tool` directly with `LanguageModelSession(tools:)`, alongside `multiTool` and `findAPIsTool`. No dedicated Multitool-side mechanism is needed for this.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `DirectToolCall.swift` and its dedicated test file are deleted.
- [ ] `MultiToolAgent.swift` is updated (not just left broken) to compile and pass its existing tests with the `callTool` turn-handling path, `directTools` plumbing, and `ToolDescriptions.callTool` removed.
- [ ] No remaining references to `callTool`/`DirectToolCall`/`directTools` exist outside historical doc-comment mentions (e.g. "formerly supported callTool") — grep confirms no live code path.
- [ ] `MultiTool.Registry`'s public surface (`affordances`, `isDirectMode`, `supportsFindAPIs`) is reviewed: if any of it existed only to support `callTool`, remove it; if it's purely about `findAPIs` vs. direct/no-discovery mode, keep it (that distinction is independent of this decision).
- [ ] `swift build` and full `swift test` remain green.

## Tests
- [ ] Confirm no test file references `DirectToolCall`/`callTool` after deletion (`grep -rn "DirectToolCall\|callTool" Sources/ Tests/` returns only historical prose, if anything).
- [ ] `MultiToolAgentTests.swift`'s existing coverage (minus whatever tests were specifically about `callTool`, which should be removed) still passes.
- [ ] Full `swift test` passes with no regressions.

## Workflow
- Use `/tdd` where applicable — this is primarily a deletion task; verify the full suite stays green after removal, and add/adjust any test asserting `MultiTool.Registry`'s surviving affordance metadata (e.g. `affordances == ["runCode", "findAPIs"]` without a `callTool` entry) if such a test doesn't already exist independent of `callTool`.

## Review Findings (2026-07-06 10:23)

- [x] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TranscriptAnalyzer.swift:250` — The `isRunCode` and `isFindAPIs` computed properties on the `AgentStep` extension are verbatim near-duplicates. Both use identical code structure differing only by the enum case name being checked. While Swift idiomatically repeats this pattern per case, the duplication could be reduced by extracting a generic helper: `fileprivate func `is`(_ casePredicate: (Self) -> Bool) -> Bool { casePredicate(self) }`, then `var isRunCode: Bool { `is`(/AgentStep.runCode != nil) }` — though this trades clarity for DRY. Accept as a language-forced idiom if clarity is prioritized.
- [x] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TurnFormat.swift:107` — ActionVerb and ActionName are near-duplicate enum definitions with identical structure but different literal values. Both enumerate the same three cases (findAPIs, runCode, final) — one lowercased, one proper-cased — creating parallel definitions that must be kept in sync. Consolidate into a single enum with computed properties: `private enum Action: String { case findAPIs; case runCode; case final }` with `var lowercased: String { rawValue.lowercased() }`, then use `Action.findAPIs.rawValue` where ActionName is used and `Action.findAPIs.lowercased` where ActionVerb is used.
- [x] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:365` — The `describe(_:)` function is a needless helper with a single call site. It wraps only a switch statement that could be inlined into its sole caller without loss of clarity. Inline the switch statement directly into the `compactMap` closure in `traceLines`, eliminating the intermediate function call.

+## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`. Deleting `DirectToolCall.swift` in this task WILL break `MultiToolAgent.swift`'s compile unless you also update it here: remove `dispatchCallTool`/the `callTool` turn-handling branch (and its `directTools` plumbing/constructor parameter) from `MultiToolAgent` now, leaving it building and passing its existing tests with `callTool` support gone — `MultiToolAgent.swift` itself is fully deleted later in `7840f24`, but it must compile and its remaining tests must pass at the end of *this* task.

If a caller wants a tool NOT meant for JS-snippet composition (rare — most wrapped tools are meant to be called from `runCode`), the answer going forward is: don't route it through `MultiTool`'s registry at all — register it as its own separate `Tool` directly with `LanguageModelSession(tools:)`, alongside `multiTool` and `findAPIsTool`. No dedicated Multitool-side mechanism is needed for this.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `DirectToolCall.swift` and its dedicated test file are deleted.
- [ ] `MultiToolAgent.swift` is updated (not just left broken) to compile and pass its existing tests with the `callTool` turn-handling path, `directTools` plumbing, and `ToolDescriptions.callTool` removed.
- [ ] No remaining references to `callTool`/`DirectToolCall`/`directTools` exist outside historical doc-comment mentions (e.g. "formerly supported callTool") — grep confirms no live code path.
- [ ] `MultiTool.Registry`'s public surface (`affordances`, `isDirectMode`, `supportsFindAPIs`) is reviewed: if any of it existed only to support `callTool`, remove it; if it's purely about `findAPIs` vs. direct/no-discovery mode, keep it (that distinction is independent of this decision).
- [ ] `swift build` and full `swift test` remain green.

## Tests
- [ ] Confirm no test file references `DirectToolCall`/`callTool` after deletion (`grep -rn "DirectToolCall\|callTool" Sources/ Tests/` returns only historical prose, if anything).
- [ ] `MultiToolAgentTests.swift`'s existing coverage (minus whatever tests were specifically about `callTool`, which should be removed) still passes.
- [ ] Full `swift test` passes with no regressions.

## Workflow
- Use `/tdd` where applicable — this is primarily a deletion task; verify the full suite stays green after removal, and add/adjust any test asserting `MultiTool.Registry`'s surviving affordance metadata (e.g. `affordances == ["runCode", "findAPIs"]` without a `callTool` entry) if such a test doesn't already exist independent of `callTool`.

While Swift idiomatically repeats this pattern per case, the duplication could be reduced by extracting a generic helper: `fileprivate func `is`(_ casePredicate: (Self) -> Bool) -> Bool { casePredicate(self) }`, then `var isRunCode: Bool { `is`(/AgentStep.runCode != nil) }` — though this trades clarity for DRY. +## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`. Deleting `DirectToolCall.swift` in this task WILL break `MultiToolAgent.swift`'s compile unless you also update it here: remove `dispatchCallTool`/the `callTool` turn-handling branch (and its `directTools` plumbing/constructor parameter) from `MultiToolAgent` now, leaving it building and passing its existing tests with `callTool` support gone — `MultiToolAgent.swift` itself is fully deleted later in `7840f24`, but it must compile and its remaining tests must pass at the end of *this* task.

If a caller wants a tool NOT meant for JS-snippet composition (rare — most wrapped tools are meant to be called from `runCode`), the answer going forward is: don't route it through `MultiTool`'s registry at all — register it as its own separate `Tool` directly with `LanguageModelSession(tools:)`, alongside `multiTool` and `findAPIsTool`. No dedicated Multitool-side mechanism is needed for this.

 title: Retire callTool/DirectToolCall escape hatch — native tool-calling subsumes its purpose
 ---
 ## What
Part of the MultiToolAgent removal pivot (see board). `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` exists today as `plan.md`'s escape hatch: a way for `MultiToolAgent`'s main loop to call one direct tool with a schema-valid argument guarantee (via a separate guided-generation grammar derived from that tool's own schema), bypassing `runCode`'s free-JS-argument path — because the hand-rolled ReAct loop's `callTool` action otherwise only ever produced a plain-language `task` description, never guaranteed-valid literal arguments.

With the pivot to Apple's real native `LanguageModelSession(tools:)` tool-calling (backed by a Router-resolved model via the `MLXLanguageModel` adapter, once the upstream multi-turn fix lands — tracked as `mlx-swift-lm`'s own task, short_id `qp8q4h9`), **every** tool registered directly with the session — not just a hand-picked subset — already gets schema-valid, guaranteed-correct argument generation as a basic property of native tool-calling itself. There is no longer a distinct "schema-guaranteed direct call" tier to escape into: it's now the *default* for any tool the session dispatches natively.

This task makes the explicit decision (per the plan's finding that this needs a firm call, not silent ambiguity): **retire `callTool`/`DirectToolCall` entirely.** Delete `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/DirectToolCall.swift` and its test coverage (`Tests/FoundationModelsMultitoolTests/DirectToolCallTests.swift`), and the `directTools`/`callTool` affordance metadata on `MultiTool.Registry` that existed solely to support it.

**IMPORTANT — `MultiToolAgent.swift` is NOT deleted until a later task (`7840f24`), and this task is NOT gated behind that deletion.** `MultiToolAgent` still calls `DirectToolCall.call(tool, task:, using: directCallSession)` directly (its `dispatchCallTool` method), holds a `directTools`/`indexDirectTools` constructor path, and references `ToolDescriptions.callTool`. Deleting `DirectToolCall.swift` in this task WILL break `MultiToolAgent.swift`'s compile unless you also update it here: remove `dispatchCallTool`/the `callTool` turn-handling branch (and its `directTools` plumbing/constructor parameter) from `MultiToolAgent` now, leaving it building and passing its existing tests with `callTool` support gone — `MultiToolAgent.swift` itself is fully deleted later in `7840f24`, but it must compile and its remaining tests must pass at the end of *this* task.

If a caller wants a tool NOT meant for JS-snippet composition (rare — most wrapped tools are meant to be called from `runCode`), the answer going forward is: don't route it through `MultiTool`'s registry at all — register it as its own separate `Tool` directly with `LanguageModelSession(tools:)`, alongside `multiTool` and `findAPIsTool`. No dedicated Multitool-side mechanism is needed for this.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `DirectToolCall.swift` and its dedicated test file are deleted.
- [ ] `MultiToolAgent.swift` is updated (not just left broken) to compile and pass its existing tests with the `callTool` turn-handling path, `directTools` plumbing, and `ToolDescriptions.callTool` removed.
- [ ] No remaining references to `callTool`/`DirectToolCall`/`directTools` exist outside historical doc-comment mentions (e.g. "formerly supported callTool") — grep confirms no live code path.
- [ ] `MultiTool.Registry`'s public surface (`affordances`, `isDirectMode`, `supportsFindAPIs`) is reviewed: if any of it existed only to support `callTool`, remove it; if it's purely about `findAPIs` vs. direct/no-discovery mode, keep it (that distinction is independent of this decision).
- [ ] `swift build` and full `swift test` remain green.

## Tests
- [ ] Confirm no test file references `DirectToolCall`/`callTool` after deletion (`grep -rn "DirectToolCall\|callTool" Sources/ Tests/` returns only historical prose, if anything).
- [ ] `MultiToolAgentTests.swift`'s existing coverage (minus whatever tests were specifically about `callTool`, which should be removed) still passes.
- [ ] Full `swift test` passes with no regressions.

## Workflow
- Use `/tdd` where applicable — this is primarily a deletion task; verify the full suite stays green after removal, and add/adjust any test asserting `MultiTool.Registry`'s surviving affordance metadata (e.g. `affordances == ["runCode", "findAPIs"]` without a `callTool` entry) if such a test doesn't already exist independent of `callTool`.

## Review Findings (2026-07-06 10:23)

- [x] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TranscriptAnalyzer.swift:250` — The `isRunCode` and `isFindAPIs` computed properties on the `AgentStep` extension are verbatim near-duplicates. Both use identical code structure differing only by the enum case name being checked. While Swift idiomatically repeats this pattern per case, the duplication could be reduced by extracting a generic helper: `fileprivate func `is`(_ casePredicate: (Self) -> Bool) -> Bool { casePredicate(self) }`, then `var isRunCode: Bool { `is`(/AgentStep.runCode != nil) }` — though this trades clarity for DRY. Accept as a language-forced idiom if clarity is prioritized.
- [x] `Sources/FoundationModelsMultitool/Agent/TurnFormat.swift:107` — ActionVerb and ActionName are near-duplicate enum definitions with identical structure but different literal values. Both enumerate the same three cases (findAPIs, runCode, final) — one lowercased, one proper-cased — creating parallel definitions that must be kept in sync. Consolidate into a single enum with computed properties: `private enum Action: String { case findAPIs; case runCode; case final }` with `var lowercased: String { rawValue.lowercased() }`, then use `Action.findAPIs.rawValue` where ActionName is used and `Action.findAPIs.lowercased` where ActionVerb is used.
- [x] `Sources/multitool-cli/CLIRunner.swift:365` — The `describe(_:)` function is a needless helper with a single call site. It wraps only a switch statement that could be inlined into its sole caller without loss of clarity. Inline the switch statement directly into the `compactMap` closure in `traceLines`, eliminating the intermediate function call.
